description:
You are acting as a performance-tuning engineer who wants to keep a repeatable
    timing record of a small demo application that already exists on the system.

    Existing resources
    ------------------
    1. Application script: /home/user/app/compute.sh  
       • It is already executable and prints a single integer result to its
         standard output when run.  
    2. You do NOT need (and must NOT attempt) to modify this script.

    Your goal
    ---------
    Automate a *single* shell command that profiles the execution-time behaviour
    of /home/user/app/compute.sh and writes the results to a log file.

    The automation requirements
    1. Create a new directory named /home/user/profile  
       • If the directory already exists, do not fail – just use it.  
    2. Run the command /usr/bin/time in its *verbose* form (option -v) against the
       application script, and redirect **both** the script’s normal output and
       the timing information into a single log file located at:  
          /home/user/profile/compute_time.log
    3. The log file’s format *must* match the rules below exactly:
       • Line 1  : The literal header string  
                   COMMAND: /home/user/app/compute.sh  
                   (No extra spaces, no leading or trailing whitespace.)
       • Line 2+ : The unaltered, verbatim output produced by  
                   /usr/bin/time -v /home/user/app/compute.sh  
                   (That is, include the integer printed by the script itself
                   followed by every “key: value” line emitted by time -v.)
    4. Do *not* wrap the time-command in any subshell that changes its native
       output ordering. The agent’s task is finished as soon as the log file
       looks exactly as described.

    Verification rules (what the automated grader will check)
    ----------------------------------------------------------
    • File /home/user/profile/compute_time.log exists.  
    • The first line is precisely  
         COMMAND: /home/user/app/compute.sh  
      (case-sensitive, no extra whitespace).  
    • Somewhere after the first line the file contains the substring  
         User time (seconds):  
    • Somewhere after the first line the file contains the substring  
         Elapsed (wall clock) time  
    • The file is not empty beyond the header line.

    Hints
    -----
    • Remember that /usr/bin/time sends its timing summary to STDERR. Use an
      appropriate redirection so both STDOUT (the integer) and STDERR (the timing
      block) end up in the same file *in the original order*.  
    • You should create /home/user/profile only if it does not already exist.
    • One single, correctly constructed terminal command is enough to satisfy
      the entire specification.

    When you are done
    -----------------
    • The only artefact the grader needs is the correctly formatted file:
         /home/user/profile/compute_time.log
    • You do *not* need to print the file contents to the console unless you want
      to inspect them for your own confirmation.
truth:
Initial state (before the agent starts):
    ----------------------------------------
    Directory  : /home/user/app
    File       : /home/user/app/compute.sh
    Permissions: 0755 (executable)
    Contents   :
        #!/bin/bash
        # Simple compute-intensive script
        sum=0
        for i in $(seq 1 100000); do
          sum=$((sum + i))
        done
        echo $sum

    Expected artefact after agent completion:
    -----------------------------------------
    File path  : /home/user/profile/compute_time.log
    Permissions: 0644 (any writeable-by-user is accepted)
    Content constraints:
      • The very first line is the fixed header string:
          COMMAND: /home/user/app/compute.sh
      • The file contains, *after* the header, the integer “5000050000”
        (the sum produced by compute.sh) on one of the subsequent lines.
      • The file contains at least these *exact* substrings (order irrelevant):
            User time (seconds):
            Elapsed (wall clock) time
      • The file is non-empty after the header.
    Note: The grader will not attempt to validate the precise numeric timing
          values—only the structural requirements and presence of the header/
          substrings shown above.
